It is split between [Cheltenham](/wiki/Cheltenham_Township,_Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Cheltenham Township, Montgomery County, Pennsylvania") and [Abington](/wiki/Abington_Township,_Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Abington Township, Montgomery County, Pennsylvania") Townships in the northern [suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") outside of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), which it borders along [Cheltenham Avenue](/wiki/Cheltenham_Avenue "Cheltenham Avenue") roughly 7 miles (11 km) from [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia"). The community is four station stops from Center City on [Septa Regional Rail](/wiki/SEPTA_Regional_Rail "SEPTA Regional Rail").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It was listed as a [census-designated place](/wiki/Census-designated_place "Census-designated place") prior to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").

**Elkins Park** is an [unincorporated community](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") in [Montgomery County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Montgomery County, Pennsylvania"), United States. It is split between [Cheltenham](/wiki/Cheltenham_Township,_Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Cheltenham Township, Montgomery County, Pennsylvania") and [Abington](/wiki/Abington_Township,_Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Abington Township, Montgomery County, Pennsylvania") Townships in the northern [suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") outside of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), which it borders along [Cheltenham Avenue](/wiki/Cheltenham_Avenue "Cheltenham Avenue") roughly 7 miles (11 km) from [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia"). The community is four station stops from Center City on [Septa Regional Rail](/wiki/SEPTA_Regional_Rail "SEPTA Regional Rail").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It was listed as a [census-designated place](/wiki/Census-designated_place "Census-designated place") prior to the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").

Historically, Elkins Park was home to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")'s early 20th century business elite, among them [John B. Stetson](/wiki/John_B._Stetson "John B. Stetson"), [John Wanamaker](/wiki/John_Wanamaker "John Wanamaker"), [Henry W. Breyer](/wiki/Henry_West_Breyer_Sr._House "Henry West Breyer Sr. House"), [Jay Cooke](/wiki/Jay_Cooke "Jay Cooke"), [William Lukens Elkins](/wiki/William_Lukens_Elkins "William Lukens Elkins") and [Peter A.B. Widener](/wiki/Peter_Arrell_Browne_Widener "Peter Arrell Browne Widener"). In the later 20th century, it was home to [Ralph J. Roberts](/wiki/Ralph_J._Roberts "Ralph J. Roberts"), co-founder of [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"), as well as to the [Gimbels](/wiki/Gimbels "Gimbels") family,[[5]](#cite_note-5) founders of the department store chain.

Today, it remains home to many [gilded age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age") mansions such as [Lynnewood Hall](/wiki/Lynnewood_Hall "Lynnewood Hall"), a 110-room, [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") estate, the [Elkins Estate](/wiki/Elkins_Estate "Elkins Estate") presently being restored as a hotel-spa, distillery and events center[[6]](#cite_note-6) and the [Henry West Breyer Sr. House](/wiki/Henry_West_Breyer_Sr._House "Henry West Breyer Sr. House"), the former residence of the ice cream magnate which now serves as the Cheltenham Township Municipal building.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 2018, *[New York Magazine](/wiki/New_York_Magazine "New York Magazine")* described Elkins Park as "an old, elegant neighborhood of close-clustered homes".[[8]](#cite_note-8) It is notable for its varied architectural styles (among them: [Modern](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture"), [American colonial](/wiki/American_colonial_architecture "American colonial architecture") and [Dutch colonial](/wiki/Dutch_Colonial_Revival_architecture "Dutch Colonial Revival architecture"), [Queen Anne](/wiki/Queen_Anne_style_architecture "Queen Anne style architecture"), [English Cottage](/wiki/English_Cottage "English Cottage") and [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_architecture "Tudor architecture")[[9]](#cite_note-9)) its wealth of homes designed by renowned 19th and 20th century architects such as [Horace Trumbauer](/wiki/Horace_Trumbauer "Horace Trumbauer"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Louis Kahn](/wiki/Louis_Kahn "Louis Kahn")[[11]](#cite_note-11) and [Robert A.M. Stern](/wiki/Robert_A._M._Stern "Robert A. M. Stern")[[12]](#cite_note-12) and its diversity of religious institutions. With six synagogues it also makes up the foundation of the "[Old York Road](/wiki/Old_York_Road "Old York Road") Corridor" of the Philadelphia area Jewish community, supported by the approximately 25,000 Jews in the Cheltenham-[Jenkintown](/wiki/Jenkintown,_Pennsylvania "Jenkintown, Pennsylvania")-Abington region.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Seasonally Elkins Park hosts a variety of religious and cultural festivals such as the "Taste of Greece" food festival,[[14]](#cite_note-14) the Romanian food festival, the Serbian food festival, various Jewish festivals such as a multi-congregation Purim celebration, and arts festivals like "Arts in the Park".[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Though distinct communities, the neighborhoods of [Melrose Park](/wiki/Melrose_Park,_Pennsylvania "Melrose Park, Pennsylvania") and historic [La Mott](/wiki/La_Mott,_Pennsylvania "La Mott, Pennsylvania") share a postal code with Elkins Park.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of 2021, the [US Census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") QuickFacts reports that Elkins Park is 62.6% white alone, 27.6% Black or African American, 4.9% two or more races, 4.2% Hispanic or Latino, and 3.8% Asian alone.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17)

The total population was 6,901 people according to 2020 Census figures.[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18)

The [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") in 2022 dollars was $121,135, the median family income was $158,000 and the median income for married-couple families was $180,189.[[18]](#cite_note-:1-18) The per capita income was $72,072.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17)

## Points of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Points of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/LynnewoodHall_front.jpg/250px-LynnewoodHall_front.jpg)](/wiki/File:LynnewoodHall_front.jpg)

[Lynnewood Hall](/wiki/Lynnewood_Hall "Lynnewood Hall"), a 110-room, [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age") mansion

* [Beth Sholom Synagogue](/wiki/Beth_Sholom_Congregation_(Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania) "Beth Sholom Congregation (Elkins Park, Pennsylvania)"), the only [synagogue](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue") designed by [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright")
* [Elkins Estate](/wiki/Elkins_Estate "Elkins Estate"), former family summer home of [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") magnate [William L. Elkins](/wiki/William_Lukens_Elkins "William Lukens Elkins")
* High School Park, an 11-acre park with four distinct ecosystems, was the original grounds of Cheltenham High School and became a township park in 1996 after the building burned down
* [Lynnewood Hall](/wiki/Lynnewood_Hall "Lynnewood Hall"), a 110-room, [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age") mansion
* [St. Paul's Episcopal Church](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Episcopal_Church_(Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania) "St. Paul's Episcopal Church (Elkins Park, Pennsylvania)"), added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1982[[19]](#cite_note-nris-19)
* [Richard Wall House Museum](/wiki/Wall_House_(Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania) "Wall House (Elkins Park, Pennsylvania)"), a house listed on State and National Registers of Historic Places, had the distinction of being the oldest Pennsylvania house in continuous residence until rehabilitation work began, now a museum

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Government")]

Elkins Park is split between [Cheltenham](/wiki/Cheltenham_Township,_Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Cheltenham Township, Montgomery County, Pennsylvania") and [Abington](/wiki/Abington_Township,_Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Abington Township, Montgomery County, Pennsylvania") Townships in the [suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") outside of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). It is represented by [Madeleine Dean](/wiki/Madeleine_Dean "Madeleine Dean") in [Pennsylvania's 4th congressional district](/wiki/Pennsylvania%27s_4th_congressional_district "Pennsylvania's 4th congressional district").

## Schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Schools")]

Further information: [Abington School District](/wiki/Abington_School_District "Abington School District") and [Cheltenham Township School District](/wiki/Cheltenham_Township_School_District "Cheltenham Township School District")

### Public schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Public schools")]

The portion in Cheltenham Township is zoned to [Cheltenham Township School District](/wiki/Cheltenham_Township_School_District "Cheltenham Township School District").

* Myers Elementary School (Kindergarten through 5th grade)
* Elkins Park School (5th and 6th grade)
* Cedarbrook Middle School (7th and 8th grade)
* [Cheltenham High School](/wiki/Cheltenham_High_School "Cheltenham High School") (9th grade through 12th grade)

The portion in Abington Township is zoned to [Abington School District](/wiki/Abington_School_District "Abington School District").

* McKinley Elementary School (Kindergarten through 5th grade)
* Abington Middle School (6th grade through 8th grade)
* Abington High School (9th grade through 12th grade)

### Private

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Private")]

* Mesivta Yesodei Yisroel of Elkins Park
* Perelman Jewish Day School
* Wyncote Academy

### Tertiary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Tertiary")]

* [Alvernia University](/wiki/Alvernia_University "Alvernia University")'s Philadelphia campus
* [Gratz College](/wiki/Gratz_College "Gratz College")
* [Salus University](/wiki/Salus_University "Salus University")
* Westminster Theological Seminary
* Yeshiva Gedolah of Elkins Park[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* The former campus of the [Tyler School of Art](/wiki/Tyler_School_of_Art "Tyler School of Art"), the art school of [Temple University](/wiki/Temple_University "Temple University"). The site is currently being developed as "a center for artists and architects" for Creatio International.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

Elkins Park is served by [SEPTA Regional Rail](/wiki/SEPTA_Regional_Rail "SEPTA Regional Rail") trains on the [Glenside Line](/wiki/Glenside_Combined_Line "Glenside Combined Line"), [Warminster Line](/wiki/Warminster_Line "Warminster Line"), [West Trenton Line](/wiki/West_Trenton_Line_(SEPTA) "West Trenton Line (SEPTA)") and [Lansdale/Doylestown Line](/wiki/Lansdale/Doylestown_Line "Lansdale/Doylestown Line") at the [Elkins Park station](/wiki/Elkins_Park_(SEPTA_station) "Elkins Park (SEPTA station)"). The [Jenkintown](/wiki/Jenkintown-Wyncote_(SEPTA_station) "Jenkintown-Wyncote (SEPTA station)") and [Melrose Park](/wiki/Melrose_Park_(SEPTA_station) "Melrose Park (SEPTA station)") stations are also found near the neighborhood of Elkins Park, and are served by the same regional rail lines. [SEPTA](/wiki/SEPTA "SEPTA") bus routes 28, 55, 70 and 77 also provide service to Elkins Park.

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Road")]

Toward the western end of Elkins Park is [Pennsylvania Route 611](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_611 "Pennsylvania Route 611") (Old York Road). In Elkins Park, [Pennsylvania Route 73](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_73 "Pennsylvania Route 73") runs along Township Line Road, mostly marking the border between Cheltenham and Abington townships.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Elkins_Park,_Pennsylvania&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

In the [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(TV_channel) "AMC (TV channel)") [period drama](/wiki/Historical_drama "Historical drama") television series *[Mad Men](/wiki/Mad_Men "Mad Men")*, the character [Betty Draper](/wiki/Betty_Draper "Betty Draper") was raised in the "tiny Philadelphia suburb of Elkins Park, Pennsylvania". During the [show's second season](/wiki/Mad_Men_(season_2) "Mad Men (season 2)"), Betty's father has a series of strokes, and is taken to "Elkins Park Hospital".[[35]](#cite_note-35) This would have actually been the former Rolling Hill Hospital, which opened in 1953, and is now known as [MossRehab](/wiki/MossRehab "MossRehab") and Einstein at Elkins Park, part of the [Einstein Healthcare Network](/wiki/Einstein_Healthcare_Network "Einstein Healthcare Network").

[Ann Patchett](/wiki/Ann_Patchett "Ann Patchett")'s 2019 novel *[The Dutch House](/wiki/The_Dutch_House_(novel) "The Dutch House (novel)")* is primarily set in Elkins Park.
